1: shadow Oh come, let us sing to the LORD; let us make a joyful noise to the rock of our salvation! The rock of our salvation — Christ: the Rock was Christ (1 Corinthians 10:4); he is my rock and my salvation (Psalms 62:2). The joyful noise is the church's praise directed at the risen Lord (Hebrews 13:15 — through him then let us continually offer up a sacrifice of praise). The singing to the LORD is the singing of the church to Christ.
2: theme Let us come into his presence with thanksgiving; let us make a joyful noise to him with songs of praise! Coming into his presence with thanksgiving — the church's gathered worship. Ephesians 5:19-20: singing and making melody to the Lord with your heart, giving thanks always and for everything to God the Father in the name of our Lord Jesus Christ. Presence, thanksgiving, and song are constitutive elements of Christ-centered worship.
3: direct For the LORD is a great God, and a great King above all gods. The great King above all gods — Christ: King of kings and Lord of lords (Revelation 17:14; Psalms 19:16). Every earthly power, every spiritual principality is beneath the authority of the risen Christ who is given all authority in heaven and on earth (Matthew 28:18). The great God is the one who became flesh and dwelt among us.
4: revelation of God In his hand are the depths of the earth; the heights of the mountains are his also. Christ holds the depths and heights — he ascended far above all the heavens, that he might fill all things (Ephesians 4:10). The one who descended to the depths of the earth (death) and ascended to the heights holds all creation in his hand. In him all things hold together (Colossians 1:17).
5: revelation of God The sea is his, for he made it, and his hands formed the dry land. The Creator of sea and land — the same hands that formed dry land stilled the storm on Galilee (Mark 4:39). Christ as creator (John 1:3; Colossians 1:16 — all things were created through him and for him) is the LORD of Ps 95 who owns the sea and formed the land. His command over creation reveals his identity.
6: shadow Oh come, let us worship and bow down; let us kneel before the LORD, our Maker! Bowing and kneeling before the LORD — the posture of Philippians 2:10: at the name of Jesus every knee should bow, in heaven and on earth and under the earth. The worship called for in Psalms 95:6 is the worship every creature ultimately gives to Christ — voluntarily now in the church, finally and universally at the parousia.
7: direct For he is our God, and we are the people of his pasture, and the sheep of his hand. Today, if you hear his voice, Today, if you hear his voice — Hebrews 3:7-8 quotes this verse as a direct word of the Holy Spirit: Therefore, as the Holy Spirit says, Today, if you hear his voice, do not harden your hearts. Hebrews applies the warning to the church hearing Christ's voice in the present moment — the today of Christ's call is the present moment of every hearer.
8: direct do not harden your hearts, as at Meribah, as on the day at Massah in the wilderness, Hebrews 3:8 continues the citation: do not harden your hearts as in the rebellion, on the day of testing in the wilderness. The wilderness hardening becomes the anti-pattern for those who have heard the gospel of Christ. The testing at Meribah (water from the rock = Christ, 1 Corinthians 10:4) is now the testing of whether one trusts Christ in the present.
9: direct when your fathers put me to the test and put me to the proof, though they had seen my work. Hebrews 3:9 continues: the Israelites who saw God's work and still tested him are the warning to those who have seen Christ's works (John 12:37) and still refuse to believe. The testing of God in the wilderness is the same as testing Christ (1 Corinthians 10:9 — we must not put Christ to the test, as some of them did).
10: direct For forty years I loathed that generation and said, They are a people who go astray in their heart, and they have not known my ways. Hebrews 3:10 quotes this exactly: I was provoked with that generation, and said, They always go astray in their heart; they have not known my ways. The forty years of wandering becomes the image for a generation that refuses Christ — going astray in the heart is the refusal to enter the rest that Christ provides.
11: direct Therefore I swore in my wrath, They shall not enter my rest. Hebrews 3:11 cites this directly, then expands it throughout chapters 3-4: the rest the wilderness generation failed to enter is the Sabbath rest that remains for the people of God (Hebrews 4:9). Let us therefore strive to enter that rest (Hebrews 4:11) — the rest entered through faith in Christ, who is himself the fulfillment of the Sabbath (Matthew 11:28-29 — I will give you rest).
